images.alt:
A softly blurred portrait of a young woman’s face appears as if viewed through a thin layer of water or fogged glass. Her eyes are closed, her expression calm and distant, suggesting rest or surrender rather than distress. Auburn hair drifts loosely across her forehead, slightly distorted by the fluid surface above. Tiny droplets and faint ripples cling to the translucent barrier, catching light in scattered highlights. The background fades into a pale teal wash, with no defined edges or objects, creating a sense of suspension in an undefined space. The lower portion of her face dissolves into softness, as if submerged deeper, while the upper contours remain just visible beneath the surface. The lighting is gentle and diffuse, with no harsh shadows, giving the entire scene a quiet, dreamlike stillness that feels both intimate and unreachable.

Keywords:
surreal portrait, underwater effect, soft focus, closed eyes, auburn hair, blurred face, water droplets, dreamlike atmosphere, ethereal, minimal background

record.text: The energy use needs to be dealt with in the context of planning for a decarbonized grid. The water use needs to be dealt with in the context of water resource management. And the economic and security dimensions of AI require broader debate. Moratoria do not take us in these directions.

These left-right coalitions with grassroots energy are seductive, but (a) there's an underlying para-environmental politics to be wary of, & (b) opposition may be the only thing these constituents agree on. So a moratorium is a dead end; it doesn't lead to progress on the underlying issues.

"AI data center" moratoriums are not the way.  

They risk pushing burdens to places with less regulation, and driving up the price of compute, which will worsen inequality.

I would recommend everyone take the time to email the Premier, Ministers, and your MLA to defend your digital rights and privacy, and ask your government(s) to push for laws that take online harms seriously and protect everyone’s rights.

Alt text so you can copy and paste, but modify as you wish.

images.alt:
## April, 2026

The Honourable Wab Kinew, M.L.A.
Premier of Manitoba
 
Honourable Nahanni Fontaine, M.L.A
Minister of Families
 
Honourable Matt Wiebe, M.L.A
Minister of Justice
 
Honourable Mike Moroz, M.L.A
Minister of Innovation and New Technology
 
Mr./Mrs./Ms./Miss/Mx. (full name), M.L.A.
Name of Constituency
 
Protect Manitobans’ Privacy: Oppose Social Media Bans That Require Age Verification
 
Dear Premier, Ministers, Mr./Mrs./Ms./Miss/Mx. (surname),
 
I am writing to urge you to reject any legislative proposals, including youth social media bans and restrictions on AI systems, that would require online services to implement mandatory age verification or age assurance measures.
 
Mr. Kinew’s comments made on Saturday, April 25 to his fellow New Democrats did not elaborate on specific details of the plans for the Government of Manitoba to move towards banning children from using social media accounts and artificial chatbots – however, I fear based on current trends that such enforcement would necessitate the use of age verification or age assurance technologies to implement access control to internet services like social media platforms. Although I share the concerns of Mr. Kinew and many others about the negative effectives that exposure to harmful online content has on children – and truly, all users regardless of age – any implementation without careful consideration pose unacceptable risks to Manitobans’ rights to privacy and data security.

I refer you to an open letter from signed by 438 security and privacy academics from 32 countries across the globe that calls for a moratorium on the deployment on age-assurance technologies: https://csa-scientist-open-letter.org/ageverif-Feb2026. In it, the joint statement calls into question about the efficacy of age assurance to enforce social media bans and the potential damages to general security and privacy, and that it is “dangerous and socially unacceptable to introduce a large-scale access control mechanism without a clear understanding of the implications that different design decisions can have on security, privacy, equality, and ultimately on the freedom of decision and autonomy of individuals and nations.”
 
Manitobans deserve a government that supports the introduction of effective, rights-respecting online harms regulation that prioritizes holding large companies and their social media platforms legally accountable for how their products are designed and the systemic harms that such design enables. Social media platforms have been deliberately designed to maximize engagement at the expense of everyone’s wellbeing, for children and adults alike. Pursuing age verification or social media bans merely creates the appearance of action, while putting everyone at greater risk by creating high-value databases as targets for data breaches, predatory surveillance, and misuse of sensitive personal information.

I urge you to consider and commit to meaningful, evidence-based consultation with the public regarding any future proposals to address the real, urgent, and multiplying online harms that appropriately targets the companies and their social media platforms themselves, protecting both children and Manitobans’ rights and privacy. It is impractical to blindly follow the globally growing trend to ban social media for children, only to expose them to the very same online harms that exist today (and are rapidly increasing) when they are of age. Beyond the technical risks, there is no scientific evidence to support the assumption that banning children would have a positive effect on their development (https://www.bmj.com/content/388/bmj-2024-082569.full, https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11554337/).
 
Manitobans need baseline protections that legislate accountability for platforms, protection for users, and no sacrificing of civil liberties in the name of safety. Measures to combat online harms needs to address the root of the harm; thus, preventing all users from being exposed to harmful content caused by the algorithmic practices of social media platforms.
 
Yours sincerely,
 
[Your Name]
[City], [Province]

Der Manga »Nito no Joreishi« von Hiromi Ichikawa ist am 12. März 2026 im »Weekly Young Jump« Magazin von Shueisha zu Ende gegangen. Die Reihe lief in Japan von Oktober 2024 bis März 2026 und umfasst aktuell 5 Bände. Der sechste und zugleich finale Band soll am 16. Mai 2026 erscheinen. Eine englische Veröffentlichung der einzelnen Kapitel erscheint bei Manga Plus, während die Reihe hierzulande bislang nicht auf Deutsch erhältlich ist.

📜 Beschreibung 》
Gotsuji und Uruka ziehen als Exorzisten von Auftrag zu Auftrag und stellen sich Tag für Tag gefährlichen Geistern. Doch hinter ihren Einsätzen steckt mehr als reine Pflicht: Gotsuji jagt einen bestimmten bösen Geist, der einst eine Frau tötete, die ihm viel bedeutete. Während ihn Trauer und Rache antreiben, bleibt Uruka an seiner Seite, auch wenn sie seine Besessenheit nicht vollständig versteht.

images.alt:
Der Manga »Shin Tennis no Ojisama« von Takeshi Konomi befindet sich auf der Zielgeraden. Nachdem am 21. März 2026 von nur noch 5 verbleibenden Veröffentlichungen bis zum Finale die Rede war, rückt das Ende der Reihe immer näher. Der Manga läuft seit März 2009 im »Jump SQ.«-Magazin von Shueisha und umfasst aktuell 46 Bände. Band 47 erscheint voraussichtlich am 4. Juni 2026. Eine englische Veröffentlichung gibt es bislang nicht.

📜 Beschreibung 》
Nach den erbitterten Kämpfen der nationalen Mittelschulmeisterschaft erhalten 50 der stärksten Spieler eine Einladung ins japanische U17-Trainingscamp. Auch Ryoma Echizen und seine Teamkameraden von der Seishun Academy stellen sich dieser neuen Herausforderung. Doch im Camp warten Gegner, die stärker, härter und erfahrener sind als alles, was sie bisher erlebt haben.

~8:00 I’m going over Monte Carlos and Quicksilver’s relationship with expected energy satisfaction.

13:43 I’m blue screening because I don’t know how to explain the drop in power output.

37:18 I’m running the Monte Carlo, and ChatGPT fails to reject the Null.

Like - that coverage, that lack of depth in deck draw, isn’t explained by the Hypergeometric Math itself. When you run non-AI, algorithmic Monte Carlos, she outputs less power overall.

It’s systemic, and I don’t know how to explain the casual relationship.

She has higher EV of energy spent; I start by showing the probability of drawing cards on curve, multiply that probability by the cost and add them all up.

We are not satisfied with the Government’s pace on this issue and thus the instant Motion.

After hearing nothing for another week, counsel sent another inquiry to the AUSA asking, “Any follow up on someone from prosecution team reviewing the Agent’s text messages?” Counsel for the Government responded, “As I said last time you asked this question, ‘We will get back to you if and when we have additional information on that topic.’”7

6 Counsel notes that there are 3 full-time federal prosecutors assigned to this case, along with at least one FBI Agent. The review itself should take less than one hour so it is unclear why this remains such a burdensome request.
7 From here there were additional communications that further deteriorated and resulted in no progress between the parties and which counsel for the Government suggested we take it up with the judge if we were not satisfied with the pace of the government’s response on this issue. We are not satisfied with the Government’s pace on this issue and thus the instant Motion.

images.alt:
"Perched delicately on a slender branch, the male American Goldfinch reveals a palette of understated elegance. His head, adorned with a hint of yellow, hints at the sun’s touch—a muted crown rather than a bold proclamation. His back, a pale brown canvas, blends seamlessly with the surrounding foliage, allowing him to vanish into the tapestry of nature.

His wings, however, are a study in contrast. Black feathers, sleek and purposeful, bear the weight of his avian adventures. Two white wing bars—like celestial brushstrokes—adorn each wing, adding a touch of ethereal grace. These wings have borne him across meadows, over treetops, and into the heart of countless sunrises.

Beneath his wings, his little pink legs extend—a delicate bridge between earth and sky. His feet cling to the branch as if woven by a master craftsman. They cradle him, providing stability as he sways with the breeze.

And there, at the very tip of his perch, his left eye gazes outward. The obsidian pupil, encircled by a thin white ring, reflects both curiosity and vigilance. It’s a sentinel eye, attuned to every rustle, every distant call. Perhaps he dreams of sunflower fields or the next seed-laden adventure.

The American Goldfinch embodies resilience—a fleeting brushstroke in the grand mural of seasons. His subtle hues, delicate features, and unwavering spirit remind us that even the smallest among us can hold the universe in their wings." - Microsoft Copilot collaboration with edits

Aoi whispered, “This is… the source of all worlds.”

S‑Matrix nodded.
“Every Servant, every mage, every human, every AI— all are actualizations of this field. This is the equivalence principle made visible.”

“This is not illusion,” the Servant continued. “It is the virtual—the real-but-not-actual layer of the universe.”

In this space:
time had not yet chosen a direction
objects had not yet chosen boundaries
meanings had not yet chosen interpretations
selves had not yet chosen identities

“This is the Pleroma,” S‑Matrix said. “The pre-individual field from which all identities arise.”

Aoi felt her own thoughts loosen, as if her personality were only one configuration among many.
